markdown:
## Product Obsolescence


<definition term="Obsolescence">Obsolescence is **when a product becomes outdated, unwanted, or no longer functional before the end of its useful life** — often by design.</definition>

<callout type="note">Designers and companies may use obsolescence to drive repeat purchases, but this can have serious consequences for the environment, economy, and society.</callout>

### Types of Obsolescence


#### Planned Obsolescence


<definition term="Planned obsolescence">**Planned obsolescence** is when a product is **intentionally designed to become outdated or less useful** after a certain period. This is done to **encourage repeat purchases**, or to allow for **new technologies or safety improvements** in future versions.</definition>

<callout type="example">**Inkjet printers with chipped cartridges**_ - _Some printers are designed to reject third-party cartridges or stop functioning after a set number of prints, even if they’re still usable.</callout>

![Planned obsolescence - smartphones with non-replaceable batteries that degrade over time](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/0ee897c87517fc2282f0470ecc64475b291ea3f5-1080x720.png)

#### Social Obsolescence


<definition term="Social Obsolesence ">**Social obsolescence** happens when a product **loses value or usefulness due to external social, economic, or cultural changes**—not because the product itself is faulty. This can include **shifts in trends, technology, or user expectations**.</definition>

<callout type="example">**Apple iPhones (annual model releases)**

* Consumers often upgrade to newer models for features or status, even when older phones work perfectly well.
* Peer pressure and marketing can make older devices seem undesirable.</callout>

#### Style Obsolescence


<definition term="Style Obsolesence">**Style obsolescence** occurs when a product becomes **undesirable due to changing fashions or trends**. However, because fashion is **cyclical**, older styles can **regain popularity**—as seen with **retro or vintage trends**.</definition>

<callout type="example">**Fast fashion from brands like Zara or H&M **_- _Clothing trends change rapidly, making last season’s styles feel outdated, even if the clothes are still in good condition.</callout>

![Skinny jeans: once a fashion must-have, now replaced by looser, trendier fits.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/279effb0e36b9f3255fdfd8b1947969835fe654e-1550x741.png)

#### Functional Obsolescence


<definition term="Functional Obsolescence">**Functional obsolescence** occurs when a product **wears out or breaks down** over time and **can no longer perform its original function**. This can happen if **replacement parts** or **essential services** are no longer available, making the product **unusable** even if it's physically intact.</definition>

![iPod Classic became functionally obsolescent when the smartphones offered built-in music storage and streaming, making single-function devices less useful. (WSJ)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/90f5292a105c19a158dd9bb94dbf058b7777dee5-1280x1280.png)

#### Technological Obsolescence


<definition term="Technological Obsolesence">**Technological obsolescence** happens when **newer technology replaces older technology**, making the old version **less useful or outdated**. As a result, **consumers switch** to the **more advanced, efficient option**, and the older technology is **no longer used in new products**.</definition>

<callout type="example">**30-pin iPod docking stations**

* When Apple switched to the Lightning connector, older docks and accessories became incompatible with new devices.
* Software updates no longer support older devices or apps.

![Technologically obsolete 30-pin docking station (eoe)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/ffd893097097019db879ba3d96018cdcdc9516f2-1024x768.png)</callout>

### Obsolescence and Responsibility


Obsolescence often leads to:

1. **Increased waste and environmental impact**
2. **Unnecessary resource consumption**
3. **Social inequalities** (e.g. pressure to upgrade)

<callout type="note">Obsolescence affects the **Triple Bottom Line** by:

* increasing waste and environmental harm (_planet_)
* placing financial and social pressure on users to upgrade (_people_)
* driving short-term sales at the cost of long-term sustainability (_profit_)

**See Section C2.1.3 for a full breakdown of the TBL.**</callout>

<callout type="note">* It's a common misconception that all obsolescence is **negative**. 
* In some cases, technological obsolescence drives **innovation **and **improves **quality of life. 
* However, it's essential to balance these benefits with **social **and **environmental **considerations.</callout>

### Ethical Considerations for Designers


  1. **Transparency**: Clearly communicate product lifespans and upgrade options to consumers.
  2. **Sustainability**: Design products with modular components or recyclable materials to reduce environmental impact.
  3. **Innovation**: Focus on creating durable, adaptable products that meet evolving user needs without contributing to unnecessary waste.

<callout type="tok">To what extent should designers prioritise **profit **over **sustainability**?</callout>
